_meta.keyKnowledge: We are learning to explain how ideas, issues and points of view in literary texts from diverse historical, cultural and social contexts — including by Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander authors — reflect their context.

_source.w: We are learning to explain how ideas, issues and points of view in literary texts from diverse historical, cultural and social contexts — including by Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander authors — reflect their context.
y: Literature is both a product of and a response to its context — understanding this deepens our interpretation.
Engaging with diverse literary contexts builds our empathy and cultural understanding.
This analytical skill is fundamental to literary study at secondary level and beyond.
eg: We explain how a novel by an Aboriginal author reflects experiences of colonisation, resistance or cultural continuity.
We analyse how a literary text's historical context shapes its themes, characters and conflicts.
We discuss how cultural values and social norms in a text reflect the time and place of its creation.
We compare texts from different contexts that address similar themes and explain how context shapes each one.
We research the social and political context of a text and present how this knowledge enriches our reading.
We identify how our own context influences our interpretation of texts from other times and places.
